title: WebJ
text:
This is a website on using Julia with Quarto. It site is hosted on github for now - so ykwongsuss.github.io. The site is similar to WebR with basic examples on dataframes, plots and some programming.

types
dataframes
tables
plots
programming


1 Websites

ykwongWebR: R Basics
ykwongWebStats: Mathematical Statistics with R
ykwongWebSurv: Survival Analysis with R
ykwongWebNPar: NonParametric Statistics with R
ykwongWebLA: Linear Algebra with Julia
ykwongWebJ: Julia Basics



2  Notes

yaml: html, pdf
qmd: footnote, references
qblocks: code blocks, tex blocks and div blocks
qtex: selected tex codes
qD2: D2 diagramming

title: About
text:
About this site

1 + 1

title: textEntry in Julia
text: For general data reading in Julia, DataFrames.jl is widely used. Additionally, CSV.jl is often used in conjunction with DataFrames.jl for reading CSV files.

title: types
text: import Pkg; Pkg.add("PrettyTables");

title: types
text:
1 Simple Data Types in Julia
The usual basic types of integer, floats, boolean, character and strings are available.

typeof(3) |> println
typeof(3.14) |> println
typeof(pi) |> println
Bool(3.14==pi) |> println

Int64
Float64
Irrational{:π}
false



1.1 Tuples, Vectors and Arrays

# tuple
a = (1,2,'3') |> typeof |> println
# vector
b = [1,2,3] |> typeof |> println
# matrix
C = [1 2 3; 4 5 6] |> typeof |> println

Tuple{Int64, Int64, Char}
Vector{Int64}
Matrix{Int64}




1.2 Named Tuple


1.3 Strings

s = "Welcome Home"
lowercase(s) |> println
s[1] |> println

welcome home
W




1.4 Categorical and Numerical Data

1.4.1 Tables

# Data
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Callie","Danny"]
marks = [84, 53, 66,  80]
grades = ['A', 'C', 'B', 'A'] 

4-element Vector{Char}:
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'C': ASCII/Unicode U+0043 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'B': ASCII/Unicode U+0042 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)




1.4.2 TypedTable.jl

import Pkg; Pkg.add("TypedTables")
using TypedTables
  tTable = Table(
    names = students,
    marks = marks,
    grades = grades
  )
  tTable |> display
  tTable[1]

Table with 3 columns and 4 rows:
     names   marks  grades
   ┌──────────────────────
 1 │ Alice   84     A
 2 │ Bob     53     C
 3 │ Callie  66     B
 4 │ Danny   80     A


(names = "Alice", marks = 84, grades = 'A')




1.4.3 PrettyTables.jl

using PrettyTables



1.4.4 DataFrames.jl

using DataFrames
df = DataFrame(
   Names = students,
   Marks = marks,
   Grades = grades
)
df

4×3 DataFrame



Row
Names
Marks
Grades



String
Int64
Char

Alice

A


2
Bob
53

4

title: types

title: types
text:
1 Simple Data Types in Julia
The usual basic types of integer, floats, boolean, character and strings are available.

typeof(3) |> println
typeof(3.14) |> println
typeof(pi) |> println
typeof(3.14==pi) |> println
typeof("a string") |> println

Int64
Float64
Irrational{:π}
Bool
String



1.1 Tuples, Vectors and Arrays

# tuple
a = (1,2,'3') |> typeof |> println
# vector
b = [1,2,3] |> typeof |> println
# matrix
C = [1 2 3; 4 5 6] |> typeof |> println

Tuple{Int64, Int64, Char}
Vector{Int64}
Matrix{Int64}




1.2 Named Tuples

# as the name implies
nt = (a=1, b=2.0)
nt |> println
nt.a

(a = 1, b = 2.0)


1



# usage
for val in nt
  println(val)
end

1
2.0




1.3 Strings

s = "Welcome Home"
lowercase(s) |> println
s[1] |> println

welcome home
W




1.4 Dict

text:
0.1 Packages

import Pkg; Pkg.add("PrettyTables");
Pkg.add("DataFrames")



0.2 Categorical and Numerical Data

0.2.1 Tables

# Data
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Callie","Danny"]
marks = [84, 53, 66,  80]
grades = ['A', 'C', 'B', 'A'] 

4-element Vector{Char}:
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'C': ASCII/Unicode U+0043 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'B': ASCII/Unicode U+0042 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)




0.2.2 TypedTable.jl

import Pkg; Pkg.add("TypedTables")
using TypedTables
  tTable = Table(
    names = students,
    marks = marks,
    grades = grades
  )
  tTable |> display
  tTable[1]

Table with 3 columns and 4 rows:
     names   marks  grades
   ┌──────────────────────
 1 │ Alice   84     A
 2 │ Bob     53     C
 3 │ Callie  66     B
 4 │ Danny   80     A


(names = "Alice", marks = 84, grades = 'A')


(Note: pretty_tables (pkg: PrettyTable) does not format well in quarto. ).


0.2.3 DataFrames.jl

using DataFrames
df = DataFrame(
   Names = students,
   Marks = marks,
   Grades = grades
)
df |> display
df |> Table

title: textEntry in Julia
text:
For general data reading in Julia, DataFrames.jl is widely used. Additionally, CSV.jl is often used in conjunction with DataFrames.jl for reading CSV files.

0.1 Data
name   sex
Alice   F
Bob     M
Callie  F
Danny   M


0.2 Matrix (by rows) to DataFrame

using DataFrames

mat = ["Bob" 53; "Alice" 84; "Callie" 66; "Danny" 80]
cnames = ["name", "marks"]
df1 = DataFrame(mat, cnames)
df1

4×2 DataFrame



Row
name
marks



Any
Any




1
Bob
53


2
Alice
84


3
Callie
66


4
Danny
80








0.3 Dict to DataFrame

dict = Dict("name"=>["Alice","Bob","Callie","Danny"],
               "sex"=>["F","M","F","M"])
df2 = DataFrame(dict)

4×2 DataFrame



Row
name
sex



String
String




1
Alice
F


2
Bob
M


3
Callie
F


4
Danny
M








0.4 Space separated-single vector (v) - Cut and Pasted
Source: Example 8.17 Devore
Data: tip percentages

22.7 16.3 13.6 16.8 29.9 15.9 14.0 15.0 14.1 18.1 22.8 27.6 
16.4 16.1 19.0 13.5 18.9 20.2 19.7 18.2 15.4 15.7 19.0 11.5 
18.4 16.0 16.9 12.0 40.1 19.2

# assign data as a single string 
# parse as a vector using split(input,' ')

txt = "22.7 16.3 13.6 16.8 29.9 15.9 14.0 15.0 14.1 18.1 22.8 27.6 
16.4 16.1 19.0 13.5 18.9 20.2 19.7 18.2 15.4 15.7 19.0 11.5 
18.4 16.0 16.9 12.0 40.1 19.2"
vec = parse.(Float64, split(txt, ' '))
vec |> typeof


Vector{Float64} (alias for Array{Float64, 1})





0.5 Using CSV
Data
ID      Test1 Test2 Exam 
SD101    11    48   61 
SD102     8    43   77 
SD103    13    15   49
SD104    15    20   57
SD105    10    40   82
SD106    11    23   65 
SD107     6    24   46
SD108    12    47   80
SD109    10    21   48
SD110     7    27   62

using CSV, DataFrames

# see CSV.jl doc under "Fixed width files"
# assign raw data
txt = "ID      Test1 Test2 Exam 
SD101    11    48   61 
SD102     8    43   77 
SD103    13    15   49
SD104    15    20   57
SD105    10    40   82
SD106    11    23   65 
SD107     6    24   46
SD108    12    47   80
SD109    10    21   48
SD110     7    27   62"

# tidy raw text to file
file=CSV.File(IOBuffer(txt); delim=' ', ignorerepeated=true)
df = DataFrame(file)

title: import Export to DataFrames
text:
For general data reading in Julia, DataFrames.jl is widely used. Additionally, CSV.jl is often used in conjunction with DataFrames.jl for reading CSV files.

0.1 1. Entered numbers

using DataFrames

a = [1,2,3,4]
b = ["M","F","F","M"]
DataFrame(a=1:4, b=["M", "F", "F", "M"])

4×2 DataFrame



Row
a
b



Int64
String




1
1
M


2
2
F


3
3
F


4
4
M








0.2 2. Cut and Pasted


0.3 3. csv

title: quartoJ
text:
Julia is supported by Quarto through Julia code chunk (type ```julia or use /julia ).
{julia}
1+1

1+1

2



0.1 Julia version
# in terminal type:
julia --version


0.2 Add Package
{julia}
#| warning: FALSE
import Pkg; Pkg.add("Distributions")

import Pkg; Pkg.add("Distributions")



0.3 Using Julia Package
Figure 1 below as an example.

using Plots
plot(sin, 
     x->sin(2x), 
     0, 2π, 
     leg=false, 
     fill=(0,:lavender))
plot!(size=(300,200))






  
    
  



  
    
  



  
    
  




























Figure 1: Parametric Plots






0.4 Using Pipe

using Pipe
# allows for _underscore as placeholder for var
@pipe pi |> round(_, digits=4) |> println

3.1416

title: textEntry in Julia
text:
2 DataFrame Data
name   sex
Alice   F
Bob     M
Callie  F
Danny   M

2.1 Matrix to DataFrame

using DataFrames

# enter data as a matrix - row wise
# enter colnames as a vector

mat = ["Bob" 53; "Alice" 84; "Callie" 66; "Danny" 80]
cnames = ["name", "marks"]
df1 = DataFrame(mat, cnames)
df1

marks

80








2.2 Dict to DataFrame

# DataFrame takes a Dict structure directly

dict = Dict("name"=>["Alice","Bob","Callie","Danny"],
             "sex"=>["F","M","F","M"])
df2 = DataFrame(dict)

2.3 Using CSV - IOBuffer
see documentation at CSV.jl

2.3.1 Columnar Data
ID      Test1 Test2 Exam 
SD101    11    48   61 
SD102     8    43   77 
SD103    13    15   49
SD104    15    20   57
SD105    10    40   82
SD106    11    23   65 
SD107     6    24   46
SD108    12    47   80
SD109    10    21   48
SD110     7    27   62

using CSV, DataFrames
using TypedTables

# see CSV.jl doc under "Fixed width files"
# assign raw data
txt = "ID      Test1 Test2 Exam 
SD101    11    48   61 
SD102     8    43   77 
SD103    13    15   49
SD104    15    20   57
SD105    10    40   82
SD106    11    23   65 
SD107     6    24   46
SD108    12    47   80
SD109    10    21   48
SD110     7    27   62"

# tidy raw text to file
file=CSV.File(IOBuffer(txt); delim=' ', ignorerepeated=true)
df = DataFrame(file) |> Table

Table with 4 columns and 10 rows:
      ID     Test1  Test2  Exam
    ┌──────────────────────────
 1  │ SD101  11     48     61
 2  │ SD102  8      43     77
 3  │ SD103  13     15     49
 4  │ SD104  15     20     57
 5  │ SD105  10     40     82
 6  │ SD106  11     23     65
 7  │ SD107  6      24     46
 8  │ SD108  12     47     80
 9  │ SD109  10     21     48
 10 │ SD110  7      27     62

title: import Export to DataFrames
text:
1 Importing .csv file

change column names with rename!


using CSV, DataFrames
using TypedTables

df = CSV.read("grades.csv", header=0, DataFrame)
rename!(df,[:ID,:Test1,:Test2,:Exam])
df

title: recode
text:
using DataFrames, TypedTables

mat = [0 31.9; 0 32.0; 1 39.0; 1 32.3; 0 41.0]
cnames = ["Disease","Biomarker"]

df=DataFrame(mat,cnames)
df |> Table

Table with 2 columns and 5 rows:
     Disease  Biomarker
   ┌───────────────────
 1 │ 0.0      31.9
 2 │ 0.0      32.0
 3 │ 1.0      39.0
 4 │ 1.0      32.3
 5 │ 0.0      41.0





df.Disease = replace(df.Disease, 0 => "noCancer", 1=>"Cancer")
df

title: wrangle
text:
using CSV, DataFrames
using TypedTables
using DataFramesMeta

title: wrangle
text:
2 Using DataFramesMeta
Use DataFramesMeta.jl for data wrangling - syntax more intuitive than DataFrames.jl See Chapter 5 of Julia Data Science

title: wrangle
text:
4 Select

4.1 Choose columns by indices

# choose rows and column by indexing
df[1:3,1:3] |> Table

Table with 3 columns and 3 rows:
     ID     Test1  Test2
   ┌────────────────────
 1 │ SD101  11     48
 2 │ SD102  8      43
 3 │ SD103  13     15




4.2 Choose columns by @select

# use select to choose columns
@select(df, :Test1, :Test2, :Exam) |> Table

Table with 3 columns and 10 rows:
      Test1  Test2  Exam
    ┌───────────────────
 1  │ 11     48     61
 2  │ 8      43     77
 3  │ 13     15     49
 4  │ 15     20     57
 5  │ 10     40     82
 6  │ 11     23     65
 7  │ 6      24     46
 8  │ 12     47     80
 9  │ 10     21     48
 10 │ 7      27     62

title: wrangle
text:
4 Filter

4.1 Subset rows by criteria (DataFrame::ByRow)

subset(df, :Exam => x -> x .< 50) |> Table

   ┌──────────────────────────
 1 │ SD103  13     15     49
 2 │ SD107  6      24     46
 3 │ SD109  10     21     48

Table with 4 columns and 1 row:
     ID     Test1  Test2  Exam
   ┌──────────────────────────

title: pivot_longer
text:
using DataFrames, TypedTables

students = DataFrame(
  math=[90,85,92],
  science = [95,88,91],
  history = [87,92,78]
)
students |> Table

Table with 3 columns and 3 rows:
     math  science  history
   ┌───────────────────────
 1 │ 90    95       87
 2 │ 85    88       92
 3 │ 92    91       78





stacked_students = stack(students, 1:3)
stacked_students |> Table

Table with 2 columns and 9 rows:
     variable  value
   ┌────────────────
 1 │ math      90
 2 │ math      85
 3 │ math      92
 4 │ science   95
 5 │ science   88
 6 │ science   91
 7 │ history   87
 8 │ history   92
 9 │ history   78

title: pivot_longer
text:
1 stack (pivot-longer)

1.1 Data1 - students

# using DataFrames

students = DataFrame(
  math=[90,85,92],
  science = [95,88,91],
  history = [87,92,78]
)
students |> Table

Table with 3 columns and 3 rows:

   ┌───────────────────────

stacked_students = stack(students, 1:3)

title: basicPlots
text:
Julia’s default Plots.jl is powerful enough to cater to many different plots, and we shall use it as our goto Plot engine for the following basic cases:

scatterplots
scatterlines
barplot
histogram
boxplot

title: basicPlots
text:
1 Plots

1.1 ScatterPlot

using Plots

scatter(iris.SepalWidth, iris.SepalLength,
  title="Scatter Plot",
  subtitle="Date: 20 May 2025",
  xlabel = "SepalWidth",
  ylabel = "SepalLength",
  group = iris.Species
  )

title: basicPlots
text:
1 Plots.jl

# Scatter plot for all trees

trees |> Table

Table with 4 columns and 12 rows:
      girth  height  age  name
    ┌────────────────────────────
 1  │ 40     46.2    15   Elm
 2  │ 45     48.2    18   Oak
 3  │ 47     53.2    34   Elm
 4  │ 51     54.2    18   Oak
 5  │ 53     52.2    28   Elm
 6  │ 55     52.0    40   Oak
 7  │ 60     58.4    50   Elm
 8  │ 63     57.8    55   Oak
 9  │ 58     60.4    60   Sequoia
 10 │ 65     53.4    50   Elm
 11 │ 63     65.0    65   Sequoia
 12 │ 70     70.0    76   Sequoia



1.1 Scatter

# using Plots

trees |> Table
scatter(trees.girth, trees.height,
  title="1. Scatter Plot",
  subtitle="Date: 20 May 2025",
  xlabel = "Girth",
  ylabel = "Height",
  group = trees.name,
  size = (600,400)
  )




  
    
  



  
    
  



  
    
  











































1.2 Scatter-Line

# Girth vs Height plots for Oak and Elm

tree2 = @rsubset(trees, :name in ["Oak","Elm"])
tree2 |> Table

Table with 4 columns and 9 rows:
     girth  height  age  name
   ┌─────────────────────────
 1 │ 40     46.2    15   Elm
 2 │ 45     48.2    18   Oak
 3 │ 47     53.2    34   Elm
 4 │ 51     54.2    18   Oak
 5 │ 53     52.2    28   Elm
 6 │ 55     52.0    40   Oak
 7 │ 60     58.4    50   Elm
 8 │ 63     57.8    55   Oak
 9 │ 65     53.4    50   Elm



# the default for Plots is a scatter-line plot 

plot(tree2.girth, tree2.height,
     markershape=:circle,
     title="2. Scatter Lines",
     xlabel="Girth",
     ylabel="Height",
     group = tree2.name,
     size = (600,400)
     )




  
    
  



  
    
  



  
    
  















































1.3 BarPlot

# using Statistics
# AvgHeight histogram of different trees

# split-apply-combine

treesGp = groupby(trees, :name) 
treesAvg = combine(treesGp, :height => mean)
treesAvg  |> Table

Table with 2 columns and 3 rows:
     name     height_mean
   ┌─────────────────────
 1 │ Elm      52.68
 2 │ Oak      53.05
 3 │ Sequoia  65.1333



# plot barPlot by trees.name

plot(bar(treesAvg.name, treesAvg.height_mean, 
         color=[:lightblue,:lightgreen,:orange], 
         legend=false, size=(400,300),
         title="Average height of trees"))




  
    
  



  
    
  



  
    
  





































1.4 Histogram

# using 500 randn (random standard normal)

using Distributions
x = randn(500)
histogram(x, size=(400,300), legend=false)




  
    
  



  
    
  



  
    
  




































































1.5 BoxPlot

# using RDatasets
faithful=dataset("datasets","faithful")

first(faithful, 6) |> Table

Table with 2 columns and 6 rows:
     Eruptions  Waiting
   ┌───────────────────
 1 │ 3.6        79
 2 │ 1.8        54
 3 │ 3.333      74
 4 │ 2.283      62
 5 │ 4.533      85
 6 │ 2.883      55



using StatsPlots

boxplot(faithful.Waiting, size=(200,300), width=0.2, legend=false)
xlims!(0,2)




  
    
  



  
    
  



  
    
  








































1.6 Curves

using Plots
x = range(-2,2, length=100)
f(x) = x^2 -5*x +2
g(x) = x^4+3

plot(x,[f,g],size=(400,300), label=["f=x^2-5x+2" "g=x^4+3"], linewidth=3)

title: wrangle
text:
3 Choose Rows with subset
Use subset instead of filter (subset works on complete columns and missing values). Syntax for subset is subset(df, function)

3.1 Subset rows by criteria (DataFrame::ByRow)

# choose rows with Exam score less than 50
subset(df, :Exam => x -> x .< 50) |> Table

Table with 4 columns and 3 rows:
     ID     Test1  Test2  Exam
   ┌──────────────────────────
 1 │ SD103  13     15     49
 2 │ SD107  6      24     46
 3 │ SD109  10     21     48



# can include the ByRow feature if needed. 
subset(df, :ID => ByRow(x -> x =="SD109")) |> Table

Table with 4 columns and 1 row:
     ID     Test1  Test2  Exam
   ┌──────────────────────────
 1 │ SD109  10     21     48
